---
title: "The Carb Education Gap"
subtitle: "Part 3 of a series on the meta-NPS. Part 2 showed the experts can't agree on how healthy a carb food is. Bring in the public and a second gap opens: people rarely see a carb food the way the experts do, and they almost always read it as worse."
author:
  - "Josh Erndt-Marino, PhD"
  - "FRESH's AI assistant"
date: "2026-06-27"
description: |
  Consumers rated 20 carbohydrate foods for healthfulness; we compared their
  beliefs to the meta-NPS. Only two foods line up with the experts. The rest
  miss, and the miss has a direction: the public reads most carb foods as less
  healthy than even the experts say. That gap, food by food, is a map of where
  nutrition education has the most to do.
categories: [meta-NPS, carbohydrate-quality, IAFNS, FRESH, consumer-beliefs, interactive]
resources:
  - orientation.html
  - misperception.html
  - disagreement.html
  - decision.html
---

[Part 2](../the-shape-of-disagreement/) ended on a hard fact: run five expert
systems across the carbohydrate supply and they disagree on most foods, grains
worst of all. That is a problem inside the science. This part asks what happens
outside it. If the experts are unsure, what does the public believe, and do the
two ever meet?

To find out, NORC ran consumer focus groups on **20 carbohydrate-rich foods**.
People rated each one from less to more healthy, then said how certain they
were. We lined those beliefs up against the **meta-NPS** — a single harmonized
healthfulness score built from five expert rating systems, the same one from
Parts 1 and 2. The comparison is preliminary and the limits from the earlier
parts still apply: no method can prove which rating is correct, so this measures
*distance between beliefs*, not who is right.

## What a misperception is

Before the results, one simple idea. Put healthfulness on a line and mark where
the experts (the meta-NPS) land. Someone who reads a food as *less* healthy than
the experts sits to the left — that gap is **negative perception** (purple).
Someone who reads it as *more* healthy sits to the right — **positive
perception** (orange). Tally those gaps across everyone and you get the food's
**total misperception**, and a single food can pull both ways at once.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="orFrame" src="orientation.html?v=5" title="Interactive: misperception as where two consumers sit on a healthfulness line relative to the experts" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:480px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

For whole-wheat bread most people land to the left of the experts, so the purple
side outweighs the orange. Those two colours hold for the rest of the piece, and
that two-part measure, sized into a single circle, is how every food gets
summarised.

## The gap has a direction

Run the comparison across all twenty foods and almost none line up with the
experts. Each food earns an *Alignment Grade* — a school-style grade on the
*size* of its consumer–expert gap, A (closely aligned) through D (far off),
regardless of which way it leans. **Seventeen of the twenty land at C or D**;
only three even reach an A or B, and just two — an apple and a soda — actually
line up. (The third, a smoothie, earns a B but, as we will see, hides a real gap
in both directions at once.) Put each food's gap on one readout, then split it
around the experts, and the shape of the misperception appears: which foods fall
to the *less healthy* side, which to *more healthy*, and how many reach both ways
at once.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="mpFrame" src="misperception.html?v=6" title="Interactive: per-food consumer misperception (Figure 1) with Alignment Grade A-D, then split around the experts line into less-healthy (left) and more-healthy (right); the potato foods fall left, honey far right, the two juices land on opposite sides, 70% of foods reach both ways, only apple and cola sit close to experts" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:600px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

The gaps lean one way. Bar after bar runs toward *less healthy than the experts*:
the public is consistently harder on carbohydrate foods than the people who study
them. Every potato food shows it — French fries, chips, and even a plain baked
potato are all read as less healthy than the experts rate them, with air-popped
popcorn close behind. The loudest gap of all runs the other way: honey, read as
far *healthier* than the experts. And the two juices flip — a sugary "vitamin C"
fruit drink earns a health halo while genuine 100% juice gets marked down. But
the deeper pattern is that most foods are not one-directional at all: **seventy
percent carry negative and positive misperception at once** — white rice splits
almost evenly, and even the smoothie carries a real dose of each. Only an apple
and a soda are read close to the experts. So the gap is not ignorance in one
direction. It is a structured, food-specific set of beliefs — a map of exactly
where the message has to work.

## And the experts are split too

It would be easy to read the last chart as the public getting carbs wrong against
a settled expert answer. There is no settled answer. These are the same 20 foods,
now showing only the five expert systems and how far apart *they* land.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="disFrame" src="disagreement.html?v=3" title="Interactive: expert ratings of the 20 foods; 15 of 20 are uncertain even among experts" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:600px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

Fifteen of the twenty earn the meta-NPS verdict **uncertain** — the label the
harmonized score gives a food when its five expert systems can't agree, their
ratings strung across the scale. Only a handful are settled: apple and popcorn
high, soda low. So two gaps stack on top of each other. The public disagrees with the
experts, and the experts disagree with one another. That doubled uncertainty,
not a simple knowledge deficit, is the carb education gap.

## Where to start

With twenty foods and two kinds of gap, the question becomes where to put limited
effort. The lever, as in Part 1, is consumption: a contested, misperceived food
barely matters if no one eats it. Filter the list down by the gaps and by how
much America actually eats each food. Watch what survives — and what does not. A
big public gap is not enough on its own: French fries are badly misperceived, but
the experts mostly *agree* they are middling, so they drop out when the last
filter keeps only the foods the experts are also unsure about.

```{=html}
<iframe class="story-frame" id="decFrame" src="decision.html?v=2" title="Interactive: filtering the 20 foods by alignment grade, consumption and expert uncertainty to four messaging priorities" style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:620px" scrolling="no"></iframe>
```

Keep the misaligned foods, then the most-eaten, then the ones the experts are
also unsure about, and four rise out of the twenty: **white rice, white bread,
corn tortilla, and vitamin-C juice.** Each is misperceived by the public, eaten
in quantity, and contested among experts. If messaging has to start somewhere,
it starts there. French fries make the point in reverse: a loud public gap, but
because the experts agree on them, the fix is plain communication, not
harmonization. Different gaps call for different fixes.

## Where this goes

Three parts, one argument. We do not have a single, agreed definition of a
healthy carbohydrate food (Part 2), and the public's beliefs diverge from the
experts' in a measurable, mostly pessimistic pattern (this part). Both of those
are features of the **informational environment**: whether we agree on what
healthy means, and whether we communicate it clearly. Most nutrition effort goes
to the other two environments, changing behavior and changing food access, on the
assumption that the information is settled. It is not. Closing the expert gap
(harmonization) and closing the public gap (communication) is lower-hanging and
high-leverage, and it feeds the rest: clearer definitions make better labels and
better messages.

Two honest limits carry over from the earlier parts. Information enables better
choices, it does not guarantee them; healthy foods are not the same as healthy
diets. And every rating here, expert or public, scores one food the same way for
everyone, when healthfulness also depends on the eater. Harmonizing the universal
disagreement is the floor; personalizing beyond it is the ceiling. This series
has been about measuring the floor honestly, so the gap is visible before anyone
tries to close it.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The 20 foods, the alignment grades, the misperception measures, and the four
priorities are from the public IAFNS *Alignment Between Experts and Consumers*
webinar and the companion analysis (Erndt-Marino & Ghirardelli, on educational
gaps in carbohydrate healthfulness). Consumer ratings came from NORC focus-group
screeners; expert ratings are the five-system meta-NPS. The numbers are a
conservative floor: only five of hundreds of published rating systems were
sampled, and a single set of cutoffs drew the lines. The webinars and papers
behind the series are on the [resources page](../resources/).
:::

```{=html}
<script>
(function(){
  var frames=document.querySelectorAll("iframe.story-frame");
  function fit(f){ try{ var d=f.contentDocument; if(d&&d.body){ f.style.height=(d.body.scrollHeight+8)+"px"; } }catch(e){} }
  function sendVp(f){ try{ f.contentWindow.postMessage({kind:"vph",h:window.innerHeight},"*"); }catch(e){} }
  frames.forEach(function(f){
    f.addEventListener("load",function(){ sendVp(f); fit(f); setTimeout(function(){ sendVp(f); fit(f); },400); });
  });
  window.addEventListener("resize",function(){ frames.forEach(function(f){ sendVp(f); fit(f); }); });
  window.addEventListener("message",function(e){ if(e&&e.data==="story-fit"){ frames.forEach(fit); } });
  if("IntersectionObserver" in window){
    var io=new IntersectionObserver(function(es){
      es.forEach(function(en){ if(en.isIntersecting){ try{ en.target.contentWindow.postMessage("play-story","*"); }catch(e){} } });
    }, {threshold:0.25});
    frames.forEach(function(f){ io.observe(f); });
  }
})();
</script>
```
